questions.question: Is there a limit on the estate tax charitable deduction?
questions.explanation: Unlike the income tax charitable deduction (which has AGI limits), the estate tax charitable deduction is unlimited. An estate can leave everything to charity and owe zero estate tax. This makes charitable planning very powerful in estate planning.
options: C) No, the estate tax charitable deduction is unlimited
A) Yes, 30% of the adjusted gross estate
B) Yes, 50% of the adjusted gross estate
D) Yes, $1 million maximum

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - The estate tax charitable deduction, as defined under IRC Section 2055, allows for an unlimited deduction for the value of property included in the gross estate that passes to a qualifying charity. This unlimited deduction directly reduces the taxable estate, potentially eliminating estate tax liability.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The 30% limitation applies to certain income tax charitable deductions related to contributions of capital gain property to certain organizations, not to the estate tax charitable deduction. The estate tax charitable deduction is unlimited.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The 50% limitation applies to income tax charitable deductions for cash contributions to public charities, not to the estate tax charitable deduction. The estate tax charitable deduction is unlimited.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - There is no dollar limit on the estate tax charitable deduction. The deduction is unlimited for qualifying charitable bequests, regardless of the size of the estate.

questions.educational: The estate tax charitable deduction encourages philanthropic giving by allowing estates to reduce their taxable value through bequests to qualifying charities. This deduction aligns with the public policy goal of supporting charitable organizations and their missions. Understanding the unlimited nature of this deduction is crucial for estate planning strategies involving charitable giving, as it can significantly reduce or eliminate estate tax liability.
questions.examTip: When calculating the taxable estate, remember to deduct the full value of any qualifying charitable bequests because the estate tax charitable deduction is unlimited, unlike the income tax charitable deduction.
questions.memoryAid: Estate Tax Charity: Unlimited!

questions.question: A "zeroed-out" CLAT is designed to have what gift tax value?
questions.explanation: A "zeroed-out" CLAT is structured so the present value of the charitable annuity equals the full contribution, leaving zero (or minimal) taxable gift to the remainder beneficiaries. If the trust earns more than the Section 7520 rate, excess passes gift-tax-free to heirs.
options: C) Zero (or near zero) gift tax value
A) The full value of the contribution
B) Maximum gift tax value for optimal deduction
D) 50% of contribution value

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - A "zeroed-out" Charitable Lead Annuity Trust (CLAT) is intentionally designed so that the present value of the annuity payments to charity equals the value of the assets initially transferred to the trust. This results in a taxable gift of zero (or a very small amount) to the non-charitable remainder beneficiaries, as the donor has effectively given away the entire present value of the trust's assets to charity.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The goal of a zeroed-out CLAT is to minimize or eliminate gift tax, not to trigger a gift tax on the full value of the contribution. A gift tax on the full contribution would defeat the purpose of using a CLAT for estate planning.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While a CLAT does provide a gift tax deduction, the term "maximum gift tax value for optimal deduction" is misleading. The goal isn't to maximize gift tax value, but to maximize the charitable deduction to offset the gift to the remainder beneficiaries, ideally resulting in a near-zero gift tax.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - There's no rule or strategy that targets a 50% gift tax value in the context of a CLAT. The gift tax value is determined by subtracting the present value of the charitable lead interest from the fair market value of the assets transferred to the trust. The goal is to have this difference be zero (or close to it).

questions.educational: Charitable Lead Trusts (CLATs) are estate planning tools that allow individuals to support charities while eventually transferring assets to their heirs. The present value of the charitable lead interest (annuity or unitrust payments) is deductible for gift and estate tax purposes. A zeroed-out CLAT is used when the donor wants to transfer assets to heirs with minimal gift tax implications, leveraging the charitable deduction to offset the gift to the remainder beneficiaries.
questions.examTip: When calculating the gift tax implications of a CLAT on the CFP exam, remember to discount the charitable lead interest using the appropriate Section 7520 rate to determine the taxable gift to the remainder beneficiaries.
questions.memoryAid: CLAT = Charity Leads, Heirs Later (with minimal gift tax if zeroed-out)

questions.question: What is the AGI limitation for cash donations to a private foundation?
questions.explanation: Cash donations to private foundations are limited to 30% of AGI (compared to 60% for public charities). Appreciated property to private foundations is limited to 20% of AGI and deductible at basis only (not FMV). This difference encourages donations to public charities.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The 20% AGI limitation applies to donations of appreciated property to a private foundation, not cash donations. Additionally, the deduction for appreciated property is limited to the asset's basis, not fair market value.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The 50% AGI limitation does not apply in this situation. While there are situations where a 50% AGI limitation applies to charitable contributions, it's not relevant to cash donations to private foundations.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Cash contributions to private non-operating foundations are generally limited to 30% of the donor's adjusted gross income (AGI). This limitation is less generous than the 60% AGI limit for cash contributions to public charities, reflecting the greater public benefit presumed from donations to public charities.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The 60% AGI limitation applies to cash contributions made to public charities, not private foundations. Public charities are generally organizations that are broadly supported by the public.

questions.educational: The AGI limitations on charitable deductions are designed to prevent excessive tax avoidance through charitable giving. The distinction between public charities and private foundations reflects the IRS's view that public charities, due to their broader public support, warrant more generous tax treatment. Understanding these limitations is crucial for advising clients on maximizing their charitable giving while minimizing their tax liability, a key aspect of financial planning.
questions.examTip: Remember the AGI limitations for charitable contributions, especially the distinction between public charities and private foundations, as this is a frequently tested topic on the CFP exam. Pay close attention to the type of asset donated (cash vs. appreciated property) and the type of charity to determine the correct AGI limitation.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'Private' is 'Thirty' (30%) for cash donations to private foundations. Public is more generous at 60%.

questions.question: CRT distributions are taxed under a specific ordering system. What is the correct order?
questions.explanation: CRT distributions follow a "worst-first" four-tier system: (1) ordinary income, (2) capital gains, (3) other income (tax-exempt), (4) return of corpus. This ensures the highest-taxed income is distributed first until each tier is exhausted.
options: C) Capital gains first, then ordinary income, then tax-free corpus
B) Ordinary income first, then capital gains, then tax-free corpus
A) Tax-free corpus first, then capital gains, then ordinary income
D) FIFO based on contribution dates

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The distribution ordering is not capital gains first. CRTs are designed to distribute the most highly taxed items first, and ordinary income generally has a higher tax rate than capital gains.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - CRT distributions are taxed in a specific order to maximize the tax benefits of the trust. Ordinary income is distributed first, followed by capital gains, then other income (like tax-exempt income), and finally, tax-free corpus. This "worst-in-first-out" approach ensures that the beneficiary receives the most tax-advantaged distributions last.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Tax-free corpus is the last tier in the distribution ordering, not the first. The goal is to distribute taxable income before distributing the corpus, which would not be taxable.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - CRT distributions do not follow a FIFO (First-In, First-Out) method based on contribution dates. The taxation is based on the character of the income earned by the trust, not when the assets were contributed.

questions.educational: Charitable Remainder Trusts (CRTs) are irrevocable trusts that provide an income stream to the grantor or other beneficiaries for a specific period, with the remainder going to a qualified charity. The taxation of distributions from a CRT follows a specific ordering rule to ensure that the most highly taxed income is distributed first, preserving the tax benefits of the charitable contribution and deferral of capital gains within the trust. Understanding this distribution ordering is crucial for planning purposes and assessing the tax implications for the beneficiary.
questions.examTip: When calculating the taxable portion of a CRT distribution, remember the four-tier system: ordinary income, capital gains, other income, and return of corpus. Always start with ordinary income and work your way down.
questions.memoryAid: O-C-O-C: Ordinary income, Capital gains, Other income, Corpus. Remember 'Oh, See, Oh, See' the order of CRT distributions.

questions.question: A client (age 65) owns a $2M company with $100K basis and wants to: (1) diversify, (2) receive lifetime income, (3) make a charitable gift, and (4) minimize taxes. Which strategy is MOST appropriate?
questions.explanation: Contributing the company to a CRUT before sale avoids immediate capital gains tax. The trust sells the stock tax-free (CRTs are tax-exempt), reinvests proceeds, and pays income to the client for life. Remaining assets go to charity. This accomplishes all four goals: diversification, income, charitable gift, and tax efficiency.
options: B) Contribute the company to a CRUT, then sell inside the trust
C) Make a bequest in the will to charity
A) Sell the company and donate proceeds to a DAF
D) Gift to charity now and take the deduction

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - Contributing appreciated property to a Charitable Remainder Unitrust (CRUT) allows for the avoidance of immediate capital gains tax upon the sale of the asset within the trust. The CRUT then provides a stream of income to the donor for a specified period or life, with the remainder passing to charity, fulfilling all four client objectives.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - A bequest in a will only addresses the charitable gift aspect and provides no lifetime income or diversification for the client. Furthermore, while it reduces estate taxes, it doesn't address the immediate capital gains tax issue associated with the highly appreciated company stock.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Selling the company and donating the proceeds to a Donor-Advised Fund (DAF) would trigger a significant capital gains tax liability for the client. While the client would receive an immediate income tax deduction for the donation to the DAF, this does not address the goal of minimizing taxes as effectively as a CRUT.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Gifting the company stock directly to charity now would allow for an income tax deduction based on the fair market value of the stock (subject to AGI limitations for charitable contributions of capital gain property). However, it doesn't provide the client with lifetime income or diversification, and may not be the most tax-efficient strategy compared to using a CRUT.

questions.educational: Charitable Remainder Trusts (CRTs) are irrevocable trusts that allow a donor to receive an income stream for a period of time, with the remainder going to a qualified charity. CRTs are tax-exempt, meaning that assets sold within the trust are not subject to immediate capital gains tax. This makes them a powerful tool for charitable planning, especially when dealing with highly appreciated assets, as it allows for diversification and income generation while supporting a charitable cause.
questions.examTip: When evaluating charitable planning strategies on the CFP exam, remember that CRTs are often the most tax-efficient option for clients seeking to donate appreciated assets, receive lifetime income, and minimize taxes. Pay close attention to the client's specific goals and the tax implications of each strategy.
questions.memoryAid: CRUT = Charity, Remainder, Unitrust: Think 'CRUTch' to remember it helps support the client's financial needs while benefiting charity.

questions.question: A donor contributes highly appreciated stock to a DAF. What happens to the unrealized gain?
questions.explanation: When appreciated securities are donated to a DAF, the donor receives a deduction for FMV (subject to AGI limits), and the unrealized gain is never taxed. The DAF is tax-exempt, so when it sells the stock, no capital gains tax is owed. This is the same tax treatment as donating directly to a charity.
options: B) The gain is recognized when the DAF sells the stock
C) The gain is never recognized—donor gets FMV deduction, DAF sells tax-free
A) Donor owes capital gains tax on the transfer
D) The gain carries over to the charity when grants are made

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The DAF is a tax-exempt entity. Therefore, when the DAF sells the stock, it does not recognize any capital gains. The unrealized gain is effectively eliminated.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - When a donor contributes appreciated stock to a Donor-Advised Fund (DAF), they receive a charitable deduction for the fair market value (FMV) of the stock (subject to AGI limitations). Because the DAF is a tax-exempt entity, neither the donor nor the DAF will ever pay capital gains tax on the appreciation. This is a key benefit of using appreciated assets for charitable giving.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Donating appreciated property to a qualified charity, including a DAF, does *not* trigger a capital gains tax liability for the donor. Instead, the donor generally receives an income tax deduction for the fair market value of the asset (subject to AGI limitations for charitable contributions).
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The gain does not carry over to the charity when grants are made. The unrealized gain is eliminated when the appreciated asset is donated to the DAF. The grants made by the DAF are distributions of the fund's assets, not a transfer of the original donated property with its embedded gain.

questions.educational: Donor-Advised Funds (DAFs) are popular charitable giving vehicles because they offer immediate tax benefits to the donor while allowing them to recommend grants to qualified charities over time. The ability to donate appreciated assets, like stock, and avoid capital gains tax is a significant advantage, encouraging larger charitable contributions. This strategy ties into income tax planning, charitable giving strategies, and overall wealth management, all key areas of the CFP exam.
questions.examTip: Remember that donating appreciated securities to a DAF allows the donor to deduct the fair market value (subject to AGI limits) and avoid capital gains tax, making it a tax-efficient charitable giving strategy. Pay close attention to the AGI limitations for charitable deductions on the exam.
questions.memoryAid: DAF = Deduction, Avoids Gains, Forever (tax-free)

questions.question: For 2026, the federal estate tax exemption is approximately:
questions.explanation: The 2017 TCJA approximately doubled the estate tax exemption, which is indexed for inflation. For 2026, the exemption is approximately $13.6 million per person ($27.2 million for married couples with portability). Note: This amount is scheduled to sunset to approximately $7 million after 2025 unless extended.
options: A) $5 million per person
B) $7 million per person
D) $27 million per married couple
C) $13.6 million per person

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - $5 million was closer to the pre-Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) exemption amount. The TCJA significantly increased the exemption, and it's been indexed for inflation since then, making this amount outdated.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While $7 million is the approximate amount the exemption is scheduled to revert to after 2025 (without Congressional action), the question specifically asks about the exemption for 2026. This option represents the sunset provision, not the 2026 amount.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - $27 million is close to the exemption amount for a married couple *with portability* for 2026. However, the question asks for the exemption 'per person,' not per couple. This option also assumes portability, which is not guaranteed.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) of 2017 approximately doubled the estate tax exemption and indexed it for inflation. For 2026, the exemption is approximately $13.6 million per person. This amount is scheduled to sunset after 2025 unless Congress acts to extend it.

questions.educational: The federal estate tax is a tax on the transfer of property at death. The exemption amount represents the value of assets that can be transferred tax-free. Understanding the current exemption amount, the concept of portability (allowing a surviving spouse to use the deceased spouse's unused exemption), and the potential sunset of the TCJA provisions are crucial for effective estate planning and minimizing estate tax liability.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, pay close attention to the year specified in the question, as the estate tax exemption amount is subject to change due to inflation adjustments and potential legislative changes. Also, remember to consider portability when calculating estate tax for married couples.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'Double Trouble' - the TCJA approximately doubled the estate tax exemption.

questions.question: The unlimited marital deduction allows:
questions.explanation: The unlimited marital deduction allows unlimited transfers between spouses (U.S. citizens) without incurring gift or estate tax. This defers taxation until the surviving spouse\'s death. For non-citizen spouses, a Qualified Domestic Trust (QDOT) is required to obtain the deduction.
options: D) Charitable transfers to be deducted from income
A) Married couples to avoid estate tax entirely
B) Unlimited transfers between spouses without gift or estate tax
C) All beneficiaries to receive property tax-free

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Charitable transfers are deductible from *income* tax, not estate tax. The unlimited marital deduction specifically addresses transfers between spouses, not charitable contributions.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The unlimited marital deduction defers estate tax, it does not eliminate it entirely. Estate tax will be due upon the death of the surviving spouse (assuming the estate exceeds the applicable exclusion amount at that time).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The unlimited marital deduction, as defined under IRC Section 2056, allows for the tax-free transfer of an unlimited amount of assets between spouses who are U.S. citizens. This transfer can occur during life (gift tax-free) or at death (estate tax-free), deferring any potential estate tax until the surviving spouse's death.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The unlimited marital deduction only applies to transfers between spouses. Beneficiaries other than the surviving spouse do not receive property tax-free solely because of the marital deduction; their inheritance may be subject to estate tax depending on the size of the estate and other applicable deductions and credits.

questions.educational: The unlimited marital deduction is a crucial estate planning tool that allows married couples to defer estate taxes. It reflects the policy decision to treat a married couple as a single economic unit for estate tax purposes until the death of the surviving spouse. Understanding this deduction is vital for minimizing estate tax liability and ensuring the financial security of the surviving spouse, a key objective in many estate plans.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, pay close attention to whether the question involves a U.S. citizen spouse or a non-citizen spouse, as the rules for the marital deduction differ significantly (QDOT required for non-citizen spouses).
questions.memoryAid: Marital = Married = Money moves tax-free between spouses (deferral).

questions.question: For portability of a deceased spouse\'s unused exemption (DSUE) to be available:
questions.explanation: To preserve portability of the deceased spouse\'s unused exemption, the executor must timely file Form 706, even if no estate tax is due. This election allows the surviving spouse to add the deceased spouse\'s unused exemption to their own, potentially doubling exemption protection.
options: D) All assets must pass to the surviving spouse
A) An estate tax return must be filed timely, even if not otherwise required
B) The estate must exceed the exemption amount
C) The surviving spouse must remarry within two years

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - There is no requirement that all assets must pass to the surviving spouse for portability to be elected. The executor can elect portability regardless of how the assets are distributed.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The election of portability is made by filing Form 706 (United States Estate (and Generation-Skipping Transfer) Tax Return) timely, even if the estate is below the filing threshold and no estate tax is due. This is a critical step to preserve the deceased spouse's unused exclusion amount for the surviving spouse.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The estate does not need to exceed the exemption amount for portability to be elected. Portability is often beneficial even when the estate is well below the exemption amount, as it preserves the unused portion for the surviving spouse's future use.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The surviving spouse's remarriage has no bearing on the portability election. The election is made by the executor of the deceased spouse's estate, and the surviving spouse's subsequent marital status is irrelevant.

questions.educational: Portability allows a surviving spouse to utilize any unused estate tax exemption from their deceased spouse. This is a valuable tool, especially for clients with assets that may appreciate significantly in the future. The election is made on a timely filed estate tax return (Form 706), even if one isn't otherwise required, and is irrevocable.
questions.examTip: Remember that the timely filing of Form 706 is the *only* requirement to elect portability, regardless of the estate's size or how assets are distributed. Don't get tripped up by distractors on the exam.
questions.memoryAid: Form 706 = 'To Elect' portability. Think: You need 'To Elect' (706) to make the portability election.

questions.question: An heir inherits stock worth $500,000 that the decedent purchased for $100,000. If the heir immediately sells the stock, the taxable gain is:
questions.explanation: Assets receive a step-up (or step-down) in basis to fair market value at death. The heir\'s basis becomes the $500,000 FMV, not the decedent\'s $100,000 cost basis. Selling immediately at $500,000 results in no capital gain. This step-up is a significant tax benefit of holding appreciated assets until death.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This answer incorrectly calculates the gain as the difference between the sale price and the decedent's original cost basis. The heir receives a stepped-up basis, making this calculation invalid.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Upon the decedent's death, the stock receives a step-up in basis to its fair market value at the date of death, which is $500,000. Since the heir sells the stock immediately for $500,000, there is no capital gain because the sale price equals the new basis.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - This answer represents the decedent's original cost basis, not the heir's stepped-up basis. The heir's basis is the fair market value at the date of death, not the original purchase price.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This answer represents the sale price of the stock, but it does not account for the stepped-up basis. Capital gains are calculated as the difference between the sale price and the basis, not simply the sale price itself.

questions.educational: The step-up in basis rule is a significant estate planning benefit under IRC Section 1014. It allows heirs to avoid paying capital gains taxes on the appreciation that occurred during the decedent's lifetime. This rule encourages long-term investment and can significantly reduce the tax burden on inherited assets, making it a crucial concept for CFP professionals to understand.
questions.examTip: Remember that assets included in the gross estate receive a step-up (or step-down) in basis to fair market value at the date of death for income tax purposes. Always consider the step-up in basis when calculating capital gains on inherited assets on the CFP exam.
questions.memoryAid: STEP-UP: Stock's Tax basis Elevates Post-death, Under Provisions.

questions.question: A revocable living trust provides all of the following benefits EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Revocable living trusts avoid probate, maintain privacy (unlike wills which become public), and allow continued asset management during incapacity. However, because the grantor retains control, assets remain in the taxable estate. Revocable trusts provide NO estate tax reduction.
options: D) Providing for incapacity management
B) Maintaining privacy of asset distribution
A) Avoiding probate
C) Reducing estate taxes

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A revocable living trust allows a designated trustee to manage assets if the grantor becomes incapacitated, avoiding the need for a court-appointed conservator or guardian. This is a key benefit of using a revocable trust.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Unlike wills, which become public record upon probate, the assets and beneficiaries within a revocable living trust remain private. This privacy is a significant advantage for many individuals.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Assets held in a revocable living trust avoid probate because legal title is held by the trust, not the individual. This allows for a faster and less costly transfer of assets to beneficiaries upon the grantor's death.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Because the grantor retains control and ownership of the assets in a revocable living trust, the assets are still considered part of their taxable estate for estate tax purposes under IRC Section 2036. The grantor's power to revoke the trust means they haven't relinquished control, thus no estate tax reduction is achieved.

questions.educational: Revocable living trusts are powerful tools for estate planning, primarily focusing on probate avoidance and incapacity planning. However, because the grantor retains control and the power to revoke the trust, the assets are included in their gross estate for federal estate tax purposes. Irrevocable trusts, on the other hand, can offer estate tax benefits because the grantor relinquishes control, but they come with the trade-off of less flexibility.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, carefully distinguish between the benefits of revocable and irrevocable trusts. Revocable trusts are for probate avoidance and incapacity planning, while irrevocable trusts are often used for estate tax reduction and asset protection.
questions.memoryAid: RIP: Revocable = Incapacity & Probate (avoidance).

questions.question: A key characteristic of an irrevocable trust is that:
questions.explanation: Irrevocable trusts remove assets from the grantor\'s taxable estate because the grantor gives up control and ownership rights. In exchange for this loss of control, the assets (and future appreciation) are not subject to estate tax in the grantor\'s estate. The grantor cannot change the terms once established.
options: B) Assets transferred are generally removed from the grantor\
s death
A) The grantor can change beneficiaries at any time
C) The grantor retains full control over trust assets

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - A key characteristic of an irrevocable trust is that assets transferred into it are generally removed from the grantor's estate for estate tax purposes. This is because the grantor relinquishes control and ownership of the assets, making them no longer part of their taxable estate upon death.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While an irrevocable trust can provide creditor protection in certain situations, this is not its *key* or defining characteristic. The primary purpose is estate tax reduction through removal of assets from the grantor's estate.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The statement is incomplete. While the trust becomes effective immediately, the key characteristic is the removal of assets from the grantor's estate, not solely the date of the grantor's death.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - An irrevocable trust, by definition, does *not* allow the grantor to change beneficiaries at any time. This lack of control is what allows the assets to be removed from the grantor's estate for estate tax purposes; retaining the power to change beneficiaries would be a retained interest, bringing the assets back into the estate.

questions.educational: Irrevocable trusts are a powerful estate planning tool used to minimize estate taxes and potentially provide asset protection. By transferring assets into an irrevocable trust, the grantor relinquishes control, but in return, the assets (and future appreciation) are removed from their taxable estate. This strategy is particularly useful for individuals with large estates who are concerned about estate taxes.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, carefully distinguish between revocable and irrevocable trusts. Revocable trusts avoid probate but do *not* offer estate tax benefits, while irrevocable trusts offer estate tax benefits but require relinquishing control.
questions.memoryAid: I.R.A. - Irrevocable = Removed Assets

questions.question: A credit shelter trust (bypass trust) is used to:
questions.explanation: A credit shelter (bypass) trust is funded with assets up to the deceased spouse\'s remaining exemption. The surviving spouse can receive income and even principal for health, education, maintenance, and support, but the assets bypass their estate entirely—passing tax-free to children or other beneficiaries.
options: B) Defer all estate taxes until both spouses die
A) Provide income to the surviving spouse while sheltering assets from their estate
D) Provide immediate distributions to grandchildren
C) Eliminate the need for an estate tax return

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While a credit shelter trust does help minimize overall estate taxes, it doesn't defer *all* estate taxes until the second spouse's death. Any assets exceeding the deceased spouse's available federal estate tax exemption will be subject to estate tax at their death.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A credit shelter trust (also known as a bypass trust or family trust) is designed to utilize the deceased spouse's estate tax exemption. The assets are held in trust, providing potential income and principal access to the surviving spouse based on the trust's terms, while simultaneously shielding those assets from inclusion in the surviving spouse's taxable estate. This allows the assets to pass to the next generation with reduced or no estate tax liability upon the surviving spouse's death.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While a credit shelter trust *can* ultimately benefit grandchildren, its primary purpose isn't to provide immediate distributions to them. The surviving spouse is typically the primary beneficiary during their lifetime, and distributions to grandchildren would depend on the specific terms outlined in the trust document after the surviving spouse's death or as permitted during their lifetime.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The existence of a credit shelter trust does not eliminate the need to file an estate tax return (Form 706) when the first spouse dies. An estate tax return is generally required if the gross estate, plus adjusted taxable gifts and specific exemption, exceeds the applicable exclusion amount. The trust's funding and operation are reported on the estate tax return.

questions.educational: Credit shelter trusts are a key tool in estate planning to maximize the use of both spouses' estate tax exemptions. By strategically funding the trust with assets up to the exemption amount, the estate can avoid unnecessary taxation as the assets pass to future generations. Understanding how these trusts interact with the applicable exclusion amount and portability rules is crucial for the CFP exam.
questions.examTip: Remember that the primary goal of a credit shelter trust is to utilize the deceased spouse's estate tax exemption and prevent appreciation on those assets from being taxed in the surviving spouse's estate. Focus on the 'sheltering' aspect for exam questions.
questions.memoryAid: Bypass = Bypass the surviving spouse's estate.

questions.question: An estate planner recommends a QTIP trust for a client in a second marriage. What is the PRIMARY advantage of a QTIP trust compared to an outright bequest to the surviving spouse?
questions.explanation: A QTIP trust qualifies for the marital deduction while allowing the first-to-die spouse to designate who receives the trust assets after the surviving spouse\'s death. This is critical in blended families where the goal is to provide for the surviving spouse while ensuring assets ultimately pass to children from a prior marriage.
options: QTIP trusts avoid all estate taxes at both the first and second death
The deceased spouse controls the ultimate disposition of the trust assets, ensuring they pass to chosen remaindermen rather than the surviving spouse\

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - QTIP trusts do not avoid all estate taxes. While the assets are deductible from the first spouse's estate due to the marital deduction, the assets are included in the surviving spouse's estate at their death, potentially triggering estate taxes at that time.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The primary advantage of a QTIP trust is that the grantor (first spouse to die) retains control over the ultimate disposition of the trust assets. This ensures that after the surviving spouse's death, the assets pass to the beneficiaries designated by the grantor, such as children from a previous marriage, rather than potentially being redirected by the surviving spouse through their own estate plan.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - This option is missing. I need the text of the option to explain why it is wrong.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This option is missing. I need the text of the option to explain why it is wrong.

questions.educational: QTIP trusts are powerful tools in estate planning, especially in blended family situations. They balance the need to provide for a surviving spouse with the desire to ensure assets ultimately pass to specific beneficiaries. The marital deduction allows deferral of estate taxes until the second death, but the grantor's control over the remainder beneficiaries is the key benefit.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, when you see a question involving blended families and estate planning, immediately consider QTIP trusts as a potential solution, focusing on the control aspect for the first spouse to die.
questions.memoryAid: QTIP = Qualified Terminable Interest Property. Think 'Qualified' for marital deduction, 'Terminable' for surviving spouse's life, and 'Interest' for the grantor's control over the remainder.

questions.question: For 2026, a married couple can gift how much to each child annually without using any lifetime exemption?
questions.explanation: The annual gift exclusion is $19,000 per recipient for 2025 (indexed for inflation). A married couple can split gifts, giving each recipient $38,000 annually ($19,000 from each spouse) without using lifetime exemption or filing a gift tax return (unless electing gift splitting).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This is the annual gift tax exclusion for a single individual for 2025, not the combined amount for a married couple in 2026. Remember to account for gift splitting when applicable.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The annual gift tax exclusion is indexed for inflation and is projected to be $19,000 per individual in 2026. A married couple can elect gift splitting, effectively doubling the exclusion to $38,000 per recipient without using any lifetime exemption or filing a gift tax return (unless electing gift splitting).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - $34,000 is not the correct amount for the combined annual gift tax exclusion for a married couple in 2026. This number doesn't align with the indexed amount for the annual gift tax exclusion.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This is the annual gift tax exclusion for a single individual for a prior year, not the projected amount for 2026. The annual gift tax exclusion is indexed for inflation and changes annually.

questions.educational: The annual gift tax exclusion allows individuals to gift a certain amount of money each year to any number of recipients without incurring gift tax or using their lifetime gift and estate tax exemption. Gift splitting allows married couples to combine their individual annual exclusions, effectively doubling the amount they can gift to each recipient. This is a key estate planning tool to reduce the size of a taxable estate.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, pay close attention to whether the question refers to a single individual or a married couple, and remember to consider gift splitting when applicable. Also, be aware that the annual gift tax exclusion is indexed for inflation and may change from year to year.
questions.memoryAid: Gift Splitting: Think 'Double Trouble' (for the IRS, because you're doubling your gifting power).

questions.question: Payments for a grandchild\'s college tuition are exempt from gift tax if paid:
questions.explanation: Unlimited payments for tuition or medical expenses are excluded from gift tax if paid directly to the educational institution or medical provider. This is in addition to the annual exclusion. Paying the student or a 529 plan does not qualify for this unlimited exclusion.
options: B) Into a 529 plan for the grandchild
A) Directly to the grandchild
D) To the grandchild\

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Contributions to a 529 plan, while potentially beneficial for education savings, are considered gifts and are subject to gift tax rules. While you can front-load a 529 plan and elect to treat the contribution as if it were made over a five-year period, it doesn't qualify for the unlimited gift tax exclusion for direct tuition payments.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Giving money directly to the grandchild, even if intended for tuition, is considered a gift and is subject to gift tax rules. It does not qualify for the unlimited gift tax exclusion for direct tuition payments.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Similar to option B, giving money directly to the grandchild, even if earmarked for education, constitutes a gift and is subject to gift tax rules. The unlimited gift tax exclusion only applies when payments are made directly to the educational institution.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - Payments made directly to an educational institution for a beneficiary's tuition are excluded from gift tax under IRC Section 2503(e). This is an unlimited exclusion, meaning there is no dollar limit, and it is in addition to the annual gift tax exclusion.

questions.educational: The unlimited gift tax exclusion for tuition and medical expenses is designed to encourage individuals to support the education and healthcare of others without incurring gift tax liability. This exclusion bypasses the need to use the annual gift tax exclusion or lifetime applicable exclusion amount, allowing for more efficient wealth transfer. Understanding this exclusion is crucial for estate planning, especially when advising clients on strategies to fund education or healthcare needs for family members.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, carefully distinguish between direct payments for tuition/medical expenses (unlimited exclusion) and contributions to vehicles like 529 plans (subject to gift tax rules and annual exclusion). Look for keywords like 'directly to the institution' in the question stem.
questions.memoryAid: Think 'Directly to the Institution' for unlimited tuition gift tax exclusion.

questions.question: To qualify for the annual gift tax exclusion, a gift must be:
questions.explanation: Only present interest gifts qualify for the annual exclusion. Future interests (like remainder interests in trusts) do not qualify. Crummey powers give beneficiaries a limited time to withdraw contributions, converting gifts to trusts into present interests eligible for the annual exclusion.
options: B) A present interest gift (Crummey power)
A) Made to a family member
D) Made to someone over age 18
C) Less than $10,000

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - To qualify for the annual gift tax exclusion, a gift must be a present interest gift. A Crummey power grants the beneficiary the immediate right to withdraw contributions to a trust, thereby converting what would otherwise be a future interest gift into a present interest gift, making it eligible for the annual exclusion under IRC Section 2503(b).
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While many gifts are made to family members, the relationship between the donor and donee is irrelevant for determining eligibility for the annual gift tax exclusion. The key factor is whether the gift is a present interest, not the identity of the recipient.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - The age of the recipient is not a factor in determining whether a gift qualifies for the annual gift tax exclusion. A gift to a minor can still qualify if it is a present interest gift, such as through a custodial account under the Uniform Transfers to Minors Act (UTMA).
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While the annual gift tax exclusion has a specific dollar amount (currently $18,000 for 2024), the statement that it must be *less* than $10,000 is incorrect. Gifts up to the annual exclusion amount qualify; gifts exceeding that amount are subject to gift tax, potentially offset by the donor's applicable exclusion amount.

questions.educational: The annual gift tax exclusion allows individuals to gift a certain amount of money or property each year to any number of people without incurring gift tax. This exclusion is designed to simplify tax administration and allow for modest gifts without requiring complex reporting. Understanding present vs. future interest is crucial for estate planning, as it impacts the effective use of gifting strategies to reduce estate tax liability.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, carefully analyze whether a gift qualifies as a present interest. Look for clues like immediate access to funds or property, or the presence of a Crummey power in a trust document, as these indicate present interest gifts eligible for the annual exclusion.
questions.memoryAid: P.A.G.E. - Present interest, Annual exclusion, Gift tax, Estate planning

questions.question: A Grantor Retained Annuity Trust (GRAT) is designed to:
questions.explanation: GRATs transfer appreciation above the IRS Section 7520 interest rate to beneficiaries with minimal or no gift tax. The grantor receives annuity payments for a term of years; any remaining value passes to beneficiaries. If assets outperform the 7520 rate, the excess passes gift-tax-free.
options: A) Transfer appreciation above the Section 7520 rate to beneficiaries gift-tax-free
D) Allow the grantor to retain full control of assets
B) Provide income to charities during the grantor\

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - A Grantor Retained Annuity Trust (GRAT) is specifically designed to transfer wealth by leveraging the IRS Section 7520 rate. The grantor receives annuity payments based on the initial value of the assets, and any appreciation above the 7520 rate passes to the beneficiaries, ideally with minimal or no gift tax liability.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - While the grantor initially funds the GRAT, they do *not* retain full control of the assets. The assets are held within the trust, and the grantor's control is limited to receiving the annuity payments. The trustee manages the assets according to the trust document.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - GRATs are primarily designed for transferring wealth to *individual* beneficiaries, not charities. Charitable trusts, such as Charitable Remainder Trusts (CRTs) or Charitable Lead Trusts (CLTs), are used to provide income to charities.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Option D is incomplete and does not provide a valid answer. Therefore, it is incorrect.

questions.educational: GRATs are powerful estate planning tools because they allow grantors to transfer wealth with potentially little to no gift tax. The strategy works best when the assets placed in the GRAT are expected to appreciate significantly, exceeding the IRS Section 7520 rate. This is connected to the broader exam topics of wealth transfer and minimizing estate taxes.
questions.examTip: Remember that a key advantage of a GRAT is its ability to transfer appreciation above the 7520 rate gift-tax-free; focus on scenarios where high asset appreciation is anticipated.
questions.memoryAid: GRAT = Growth Rate Above Threshold (7520 rate) Transfers

questions.question: A cross-purchase buy-sell agreement:
questions.explanation: In a cross-purchase agreement, surviving owners (not the company) purchase the deceased owner\'s business interest. Each owner owns life insurance on the other owners. This provides a step-up in basis for the surviving owners. With many owners, an entity purchase or trusteed arrangement may be simpler.
options: A) Has the company purchase a deceased owner\
s interest
C) Requires the estate to purchase new ownership interests

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - In a cross-purchase agreement, the company is not involved in purchasing the deceased owner's shares. This is characteristic of an entity purchase (redemption) agreement, not a cross-purchase agreement.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - This option is incomplete and doesn't describe any type of buy-sell agreement. It lacks the essential element of who is purchasing the deceased owner's interest.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - In a cross-purchase buy-sell agreement, the surviving owners individually purchase the deceased owner's share of the business. Each owner typically holds life insurance policies on the other owners to fund this purchase, ensuring a smooth transfer of ownership.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This option is incomplete and doesn't describe any type of buy-sell agreement. It lacks the essential element of who is purchasing the deceased owner's interest.

questions.educational: Buy-sell agreements are crucial for business succession planning, providing a predetermined method for transferring ownership upon the death, disability, or retirement of an owner. Cross-purchase agreements are often favored when there are fewer owners because they allow the surviving owners to receive a step-up in basis for the purchased shares, potentially reducing future capital gains taxes. This contrasts with entity purchase agreements where the company buys back the shares, which does not provide a basis increase to the remaining owners.
questions.examTip: When analyzing buy-sell agreement questions, immediately identify whether the agreement is cross-purchase or entity purchase, as this dictates who is buying the shares and the tax implications for the owners.
questions.memoryAid: Cross-Purchase: Owners CROSS the street to buy each other's shares.

questions.question: Minority and lack of marketability discounts can reduce the gift tax value of transferred business interests because:
questions.explanation: Minority interests lack control over business decisions, and interests in closely-held businesses lack ready markets for sale. These factors make such interests worth less than their proportionate share of total business value. Properly documented discounts (typically 20-40% combined) can significantly reduce gift and estate tax on transfers.
options: B) A minority, non-marketable interest is worth less than a proportionate share of total value
D) Discounts only apply to C corporation interests
A) All business interests are automatically discounted
C) The IRS requires discounts on all transfers

whyWrong.0: Why option A is CORRECT - A minority interest holder lacks control over the business, and a lack of marketability means there's no easy way to sell the interest. Therefore, a willing buyer would pay less for such an interest than its proportionate share of the total business value, justifying valuation discounts for gift and estate tax purposes.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Discounts for minority interest and lack of marketability can apply to various business entities, including S corporations, LLCs, and partnerships, not just C corporations. The type of entity affects valuation methods, but not the fundamental applicability of discounts.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Discounts are not automatically applied; they must be supported by a qualified appraisal that demonstrates the reduced value due to minority interest and lack of marketability. The IRS scrutinizes these discounts, so proper documentation is crucial.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The IRS does not require discounts; rather, taxpayers may claim discounts if they can demonstrate that the fair market value of the transferred interest is less than its pro rata share of the business's total value due to minority interest and lack of marketability. The burden of proof lies with the taxpayer.

questions.educational: Minority and lack of marketability discounts are permitted because fair market value is defined as what a willing buyer would pay a willing seller, both having reasonable knowledge of the relevant facts. A buyer would pay less for an illiquid, non-controlling interest. These discounts are important tools in business succession planning, allowing for the transfer of business interests at a lower tax cost, facilitating the continuation of the business within the family or other desired parties.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, remember that valuation discounts are fact-specific and require qualified appraisals. Be wary of questions that suggest automatic or guaranteed discounts.
questions.memoryAid: MiLM – Minority Interest, Lack of Marketability: These factors LOWER the value.

questions.question: A will becomes effective:
questions.explanation: A will is ambulatory—it has no effect until death and can be changed anytime before death. It becomes effective upon the testator\'s death, at which point it is admitted to probate. During life, the testator retains full control over assets and can revoke or amend the will.
options: B) When witnessed
D) When filed with the probate court
A) When signed by the testator
C) Upon the testator\

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A will must be witnessed to be valid, but witnessing alone does not make it effective. The will remains ambulatory until the testator's death.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Filing a will with the probate court typically occurs *after* the testator's death, as part of the probate process. The will does not become effective upon filing; it becomes effective upon death and is then filed.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Signing a will is a necessary step for its validity, but the will remains a revocable document until the testator's death. The testator can change or revoke the will at any time before death, meaning it's not effective simply upon signing.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - A will is an ambulatory document, meaning it has no legal effect until the testator's death. Upon death, the will becomes operative, dictating the distribution of the testator's assets according to its terms, subject to the probate process.

questions.educational: The ambulatory nature of a will is fundamental to estate planning. It allows individuals to adapt their estate plans to changing circumstances (e.g., births, deaths, divorces, asset fluctuations) throughout their lives. This flexibility is crucial because estate planning is a dynamic process that should reflect the testator's current wishes and financial situation, aligning with the CFP Board's emphasis on comprehensive financial planning.
questions.examTip: Remember that a will is ambulatory, meaning it can be changed or revoked at any time before death; on the CFP exam, consider how this impacts planning for incapacity and potential will contests.
questions.memoryAid: A will is like an ambulance: it doesn't help until someone is dead (testator's death).

questions.question: A durable power of attorney differs from a regular power of attorney in that it:
questions.explanation: A durable power of attorney includes language that it continues in effect (or becomes effective) if the principal becomes incapacitated. A regular POA becomes void upon incapacity. This is essential for managing affairs if someone becomes unable to handle their own decisions.
options: D) Automatically expires after one year
B) Continues to be effective if the principal becomes incapacitated
A) Only covers financial matters
C) Cannot be revoked

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A durable power of attorney does not automatically expire after one year. Its duration is determined by the terms specified within the document, and it can last for many years or until revoked by the principal (if competent) or upon the principal's death.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - The key difference between a durable power of attorney and a regular power of attorney is its survivability of the principal's incapacity. A durable power of attorney contains specific language that allows it to remain effective even if the principal becomes incapacitated, whereas a regular power of attorney terminates automatically upon the principal's incapacity.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While a power of attorney *can* be limited to financial matters, it is not inherently limited to financial matters. A power of attorney, durable or not, can be drafted to cover healthcare decisions (healthcare power of attorney) or both financial and healthcare decisions.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - A durable power of attorney can be revoked by the principal as long as the principal is of sound mind and not under duress. The ability to revoke is a fundamental right, ensuring the principal retains control over their affairs as long as they are capable.

questions.educational: Durable powers of attorney are crucial estate planning tools because they allow for the continued management of a person's affairs even if they become unable to manage them personally due to illness or injury. This avoids the need for a potentially costly and time-consuming guardianship or conservatorship proceeding. Understanding the nuances of powers of attorney is essential for providing comprehensive financial planning advice, particularly in the context of retirement and long-term care planning.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, pay close attention to the wording of power of attorney questions, specifically looking for terms like 'durable,' 'incapacity,' and 'springing' to identify the correct answer. Remember that a durable POA addresses the potential for future incapacity.
questions.memoryAid: Durable = Doesn't Die with Disability

questions.question: A healthcare power of attorney allows the appointed agent to:
questions.explanation: A healthcare power of attorney (healthcare proxy) authorizes an agent to make medical decisions when the principal cannot. This is separate from financial powers of attorney. A living will/advance directive may also specify end-of-life treatment preferences.
options: A) Make financial decisions for the principal
B) Make medical decisions if the principal cannot
D) Manage the principal\
s will

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A healthcare power of attorney specifically addresses healthcare decisions. Financial decisions are handled by a durable power of attorney for finances, a separate legal document.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A healthcare power of attorney (also known as a healthcare proxy) grants an agent the authority to make medical decisions on behalf of the principal if the principal is unable to make those decisions themselves due to incapacitation or other reasons. This ensures the principal's wishes are honored even when they cannot communicate them directly.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While a healthcare power of attorney indirectly involves managing the principal's healthcare, it doesn't grant broad management powers over all aspects of the principal's life. The agent's authority is limited to healthcare decisions.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Option D is incomplete and does not represent a valid choice.

questions.educational: Healthcare powers of attorney are crucial components of a comprehensive estate plan, ensuring that an individual's medical wishes are respected even when they lack the capacity to communicate them. They work in conjunction with other documents like living wills (advance directives) to provide clear guidance to medical professionals and loved ones. Understanding the distinctions between healthcare and financial powers of attorney is vital for effective client planning.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, pay close attention to the specific wording of questions related to powers of attorney. Differentiate between healthcare and financial powers, and remember that a healthcare power of attorney only applies when the principal is incapacitated.
questions.memoryAid: Healthcare = Health Choices

questions.question: A special needs trust is designed to:
questions.explanation: Special needs trusts (supplemental needs trusts) provide for a disabled beneficiary\'s needs beyond what government programs cover without disqualifying them from means-tested benefits like Medicaid and SSI. The trust must be properly structured to avoid being counted as the beneficiary\'s assets.
options: B) Reduce estate taxes for high-net-worth individuals
A) Provide for a beneficiary without disqualifying them from government benefits
D) Hold life insurance policies
C) Fund college education expenses

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - While trusts can be used to reduce estate taxes, a special needs trust's primary purpose is not estate tax reduction. Its main goal is to provide for a disabled beneficiary without jeopardizing their eligibility for needs-based government benefits. Estate tax planning is a separate, albeit sometimes related, consideration.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Special needs trusts, also known as supplemental needs trusts, are designed to provide for the needs of a disabled beneficiary beyond what is covered by government benefits like Medicaid and Supplemental Security Income (SSI). These trusts are carefully structured to avoid being counted as available resources for the beneficiary, thus preserving their eligibility for these crucial means-tested programs.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While life insurance policies can be owned by a trust, including an irrevocable life insurance trust (ILIT) for estate tax purposes, a special needs trust is not specifically designed to hold life insurance. The primary purpose remains providing for the beneficiary's supplemental needs without affecting government benefits.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While trusts can be used to fund college education expenses (e.g., 529 plans held in trust), a special needs trust is not designed for this purpose. Its focus is on providing for the ongoing care and support of a disabled individual, not specifically educational expenses. Other types of trusts, like education trusts, are more suitable for college funding.

questions.educational: Special needs trusts are crucial in estate planning for families with disabled dependents. These trusts allow individuals to enhance the quality of life for their loved ones without jeopardizing their access to essential government assistance programs. Understanding the nuances of special needs trusts is vital for CFPs, as they often encounter clients seeking to provide long-term care and financial security for family members with disabilities, while navigating complex eligibility rules for means-tested benefits.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, pay close attention to the beneficiary's circumstances when a trust is involved. If the beneficiary is disabled and receiving government benefits, a special needs trust is likely the most appropriate solution.
questions.memoryAid: SNT: 'Supplemental Needs Trust' - remember it supplements, not supplants, government benefits.

questions.question: An Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust (ILIT) removes life insurance proceeds from the taxable estate if:
questions.explanation: For life insurance to be excluded from the estate, the insured must have no incidents of ownership (ability to change beneficiary, borrow against policy, etc.). The ILIT must own the policy, pay premiums, and the insured should not be trustee. If a policy is transferred to an ILIT, the three-year rule applies.
options: D) The insured is also the trustee
A) The insured names the trust as beneficiary
B) The trust owns the policy and the insured has no incidents of ownership
C) The policy was purchased within three years of death

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - If the insured is also the trustee, they retain incidents of ownership, causing the life insurance proceeds to be included in their gross estate. The insured serving as trustee gives them control over the policy, defeating the purpose of removing it from the estate.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Naming the trust as beneficiary is necessary, but not sufficient, to remove life insurance proceeds from the taxable estate. The insured must also relinquish all incidents of ownership in the policy for it to be excluded.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - For life insurance proceeds to be excluded from the insured's gross estate, the ILIT must own the policy, and the insured must relinquish all incidents of ownership. This ensures the insured has no control over the policy or its proceeds, effectively removing it from their estate for estate tax purposes.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - The policy being purchased within three years of death is related to the *transfer* of an existing policy into an ILIT. If the insured transfers a policy they already own and die within three years, the proceeds are included in the estate. However, if the ILIT *initially purchases* the policy, the three-year rule does not apply.

questions.educational: An Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust (ILIT) is a powerful estate planning tool used to remove life insurance proceeds from your taxable estate. The key is to ensure the insured retains no incidents of ownership, such as the right to change beneficiaries or borrow against the policy. This is because estate tax is levied on assets the deceased owned or controlled at the time of death.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, pay close attention to who owns the life insurance policy and who has the power to make changes to it. If the insured retains any control, the proceeds will likely be included in their estate.
questions.memoryAid: ILIT = Insured Loses Incidents (of ownership) = Tax savings.

questions.question: A Charitable Remainder Trust (CRT) provides:
questions.explanation: CRTs provide income to the donor or other non-charitable beneficiaries for a term or life, with the remainder passing to charity. The donor receives a current income tax deduction for the present value of the charitable remainder. CRTs work well with highly appreciated assets.
options: A) Immediate income to charity with remainder to family
B) Income to the donor/family for life with remainder to charity
D) Assets that remain in the donor\

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - A Charitable Remainder Trust (CRT) is designed to benefit a non-charitable beneficiary (often the donor) with income first, and the charity receives the remainder interest. This option reverses that order, which is not how a CRT functions.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A Charitable Remainder Trust (CRT) is an irrevocable trust that provides a stream of income to a non-charitable beneficiary (often the donor or their family) for a specific term or for life, after which the remaining assets in the trust are distributed to a designated charity. This structure allows for a current income tax deduction based on the present value of the charity's remainder interest.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While the donor initially contributes assets to the CRT, the assets do *not* remain in the donor's estate. The assets are held within the irrevocable trust and eventually pass to the designated charity, removing them from the donor's taxable estate.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Option D is incomplete and doesn't present a coherent statement about Charitable Remainder Trusts. It cannot be a correct answer.

questions.educational: Charitable Remainder Trusts (CRTs) are powerful estate planning tools used to benefit both the donor and a charity. They allow a donor to receive income from appreciated assets while also receiving a current income tax deduction and reducing potential estate taxes. The trade-off is giving up ultimate ownership of the asset to the charity.
questions.examTip: When calculating the income tax deduction for a CRT, remember to consider the applicable federal rate (AFR) and the payout rate, as these factors significantly impact the present value of the charitable remainder interest.
questions.memoryAid: CRT: Charity Receives Trust (Remainder).

questions.question: The Generation-Skipping Transfer Tax (GSTT) applies to:
questions.explanation: GSTT is a separate tax on transfers to skip persons (generally grandchildren or those 37.5+ years younger than the donor). It prevents avoidance of one generation of transfer tax. The GSTT rate is a flat 40%, in addition to any estate or gift tax. Each person has a GSTT exemption equal to the estate exemption.
options: A) All transfers to children
D) Only outright gifts, not trusts
B) Transfers to grandchildren or lower generations that skip a generation
C) Transfers between spouses

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The Generation-Skipping Transfer Tax (GSTT) does not apply to all transfers to children. It only applies when assets are transferred to individuals two or more generations younger than the transferor, effectively skipping a generation for tax purposes.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The GSTT applies to both outright gifts and transfers made through trusts. The type of transfer vehicle does not determine GSTT applicability; rather, it is the generational relationship between the transferor and the transferee.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The Generation-Skipping Transfer Tax (GSTT) is designed to prevent the avoidance of estate tax by transferring wealth directly to grandchildren or other 'skip persons' (individuals two or more generations younger than the transferor). This ensures that wealth is taxed at least once per generation.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Transfers between spouses generally qualify for the unlimited marital deduction and are therefore exempt from both gift and estate taxes, as well as the Generation-Skipping Transfer Tax. The GSTT focuses on transfers that bypass a generation, not spousal transfers.

questions.educational: The Generation-Skipping Transfer Tax (GSTT) exists to prevent wealthy families from avoiding estate taxes by transferring assets directly to grandchildren or more distant descendants. Without the GSTT, a generation's estate tax could be bypassed. Understanding the GSTT is crucial for comprehensive estate planning, especially when dealing with large estates and complex family structures, as it directly impacts the net amount ultimately received by beneficiaries.
questions.examTip: When calculating estate tax, remember to consider the potential impact of the GSTT in addition to federal estate and gift taxes, especially when clients have grandchildren or other distant relatives they wish to benefit.
questions.memoryAid: GSTT: Grandchildren Skipping Taxes Triggered

questions.question: A married couple wants to use gift splitting. One spouse makes an $80,000 gift to their adult child. If both spouses elect gift splitting and the annual exclusion is $18,000, what is the taxable gift amount?
questions.explanation: With gift splitting, the $80,000 gift is treated as if each spouse gave $40,000. Each spouse applies their $18,000 annual exclusion, reducing the taxable portion to $22,000 each, or $44,000 total. Gift splitting requires both spouses to consent and file a gift tax return.
options: $80,000 because gift splitting does not apply to gifts exceeding $50,000
$44,000 because the gift is split equally and each spouse applies their $18,000 annual exclusion
$62,000 because only one spouse\

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Gift splitting is permitted regardless of the gift's size, as long as both spouses consent and are legally married at the time of the gift. The $50,000 limit is not relevant to gift splitting rules.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Gift splitting allows a married couple to treat a gift made by one spouse as if each spouse made one-half of the gift. In this case, the $80,000 gift is split into two $40,000 gifts. Each spouse can then apply their $18,000 annual exclusion, resulting in a taxable gift of $22,000 per spouse, or $44,000 total ($22,000 x 2 = $44,000).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Gift splitting requires the consent of both spouses. The calculation is incorrect because it does not account for the gift being split and both spouses using their annual exclusion.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This option is incomplete and does not provide a valid answer. Therefore, it cannot be the correct choice.

questions.educational: Gift splitting is a valuable estate planning tool that allows married couples to maximize their annual gift tax exclusions and unified credit. By treating a gift as if it were made equally by both spouses, they can effectively double the amount that can be gifted tax-free each year. This strategy is particularly useful when one spouse has significantly more assets than the other.
questions.examTip: Remember that gift splitting requires the consent of both spouses and the filing of a gift tax return (Form 709) even if no gift tax is ultimately due. Be sure to calculate the taxable gift amount per spouse when gift splitting is involved.
questions.memoryAid: Gift SPLIT: Spouses Pool Limit Increases Together

questions.question: The estate tax charitable deduction is:
questions.explanation: Unlike income tax charitable deductions, the estate tax charitable deduction is unlimited. Assets passing to qualified charities at death are fully deductible, reducing the taxable estate dollar-for-dollar. This makes charitable bequests a powerful estate tax planning tool.
options: D) Phased out for large estates
A) Limited to 30% of the gross estate
B) Unlimited for qualifying charitable transfers
C) Only available for transfers to private foundations

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - The estate tax charitable deduction is *not* phased out for large estates. It remains unlimited regardless of the estate's size, making it a valuable tool for even the wealthiest individuals.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - The estate tax charitable deduction is *not* limited to 30% of the gross estate. This 30% limitation applies to certain *income tax* charitable deductions, not estate tax deductions.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - The estate tax charitable deduction is unlimited for qualifying charitable transfers. This means the entire value of assets passing to qualified charities is deductible from the gross estate, reducing the taxable estate and potentially the estate tax liability. A 'qualified charity' generally refers to organizations described in IRC Section 501(c)(3).
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - While transfers to private foundations *can* qualify for the estate tax charitable deduction, it is *not* limited to them. Transfers to a wide range of qualified charities, including public charities, also qualify.

questions.educational: The unlimited estate tax charitable deduction encourages philanthropic giving as part of estate planning. By donating to qualified charities, individuals can reduce their estate tax liability, aligning their values with their financial planning. This deduction is a key component of estate tax minimization strategies, alongside tools like trusts and gifting.
questions.examTip: On the CFP exam, carefully distinguish between income tax and estate tax rules regarding charitable deductions, as the limitations and qualifications differ significantly.
questions.memoryAid: Estate Tax Charitable Deduction: 'Unlimited Love' (Unlimited deduction for charitable bequests).

questions.question: Which asset is NOT typically included in the gross estate?
questions.explanation: Life insurance owned by an ILIT (not by the decedent) is not included in the gross estate because the decedent had no incidents of ownership. Life insurance owned by the decedent, jointly-held property, and revocable trust assets are all included in the gross estate.
options: D) Revocable trust assets
B) Jointly-held property with right of survivorship
A) Life insurance owned by the decedent on their own life
C) Life insurance on the decedent\

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - Assets held in a revocable trust are included in the gross estate because the grantor (the decedent) retained control and the power to alter, amend, or revoke the trust, making it essentially an extension of their estate.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - Jointly-held property with right of survivorship is generally included in the gross estate. The portion included depends on the relationship between the joint owners. If between spouses, generally 50% is included, regardless of contribution. If not spouses, the full value is included unless the surviving owner can prove contribution.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - Life insurance proceeds are included in the gross estate if the decedent owned the policy on their own life or if the proceeds are payable to the decedent's estate. This is because the decedent had incidents of ownership, such as the right to change the beneficiary or borrow against the policy.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - The question is negatively worded, asking which asset is NOT typically included. Life insurance on the decedent, without specifying ownership, is ambiguous. If the life insurance is owned by someone else (like an Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust or ILIT) and the decedent had no incidents of ownership, the proceeds are NOT included in the gross estate. The key is ownership and incidents of ownership.

questions.educational: The gross estate is the starting point for determining estate tax liability. It includes the value of all property owned by the decedent at the time of death, plus certain property interests the decedent transferred during life but retained control over. Understanding what constitutes the gross estate is crucial for calculating the taxable estate and planning for estate tax minimization, a core function of financial planning.
questions.examTip: Pay close attention to the wording of estate tax questions, especially regarding ownership and incidents of ownership. Negatively worded questions are common, so double-check what the question is asking you to identify.
questions.memoryAid: GRATs and ILITs get assets out of the estate. Retained control (revocable trusts) keeps assets in the estate.

questions.question: Estate liquidity planning is important because:
questions.explanation: Estates often contain illiquid assets (real estate, business interests) but face liquidity needs for taxes, administration costs, and family support. Without planning (life insurance, installment payments under §6166), heirs may be forced to sell illiquid assets quickly at discounted prices.
options: D) Only liquid assets are subject to estate tax
B) Illiquid assets may need to be sold at disadvantageous prices to pay taxes and expenses
A) All estates must pay taxes within 30 days
C) Probate courts require cash deposits

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - All assets in the gross estate, not just liquid assets, are subject to estate tax. The estate tax is calculated on the fair market value of all assets owned by the decedent at the time of death, including real estate, stocks, bonds, and personal property.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Estate liquidity planning addresses the potential need to sell illiquid assets, such as real estate or closely held business interests, to cover estate taxes, debts, and administrative expenses. Without proper planning, these assets may have to be sold quickly, potentially resulting in lower prices and reduced value for the heirs.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - While estate taxes are due nine months after the date of death (not 30 days), this statement is incorrect because not all estates are required to pay estate taxes. The applicable exclusion amount (currently $13.61 million per individual for 2024) shields many estates from federal estate tax.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - Probate courts do not generally require cash deposits from estates. While there may be court fees associated with the probate process, these are typically paid from the estate's assets and are not required as an upfront deposit.

questions.educational: Estate liquidity planning is crucial because the estate tax and other expenses (funeral costs, debts, administration) must be paid in a timely manner. Without sufficient liquid assets, the estate may be forced to sell illiquid assets at fire-sale prices, diminishing the inheritance for beneficiaries.
